text:
GENITOURINARY MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
FOLLOW-UP OFFICE VISIT — METASTATIC CASTRATION-RESISTANT PROSTATE CANCER

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending]
DEPARTMENT: Genitourinary Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Kowalski is a 70-year-old Polish-American male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC, Gleason 9, BRCA2 germline and somatic biallelic loss) who returns today for a follow-up visit, approximately 6 months into his second-line systemic therapy with enzalutamide. He presents with a rising PSA and new onset right hip pain, in addition to worsening low back pain.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately 2 years ago with de novo metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), Gleason 9 (5+4), Grade Group 5, cT3bN1M1b with bone metastases at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala, plus pelvic lymphadenopathy. His PSA at diagnosis was 187.4 ng/mL. He was initiated on ADT with leuprolide acetate plus abiraterone/prednisone (Zytiga) per the LATITUDE/STAMPEDE paradigm. He had a robust initial PSA response, reaching a nadir of 0.8 ng/mL at approximately 12 months.

His PSA subsequently rose, and by 24 months from initial therapy he had met criteria for castration resistance: three consecutive PSA rises above nadir with a confirmed castrate testosterone level below 10 ng/dL, with a PSA doubling time of approximately 3.5 months. He also developed new low back pain at that time. Germline genetic testing performed earlier in his course revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift variant (c.5946delT). Somatic tumor profiling of a CT-guided biopsy of the L3 vertebral metastasis confirmed BRCA2 biallelic loss (deep deletion, consistent with LOH of the germline allele plus somatic second hit), along with AR amplification (~8x), TP53 p.R175H, RB1 homozygous deletion, PTEN loss, and MYC amplification. MSI was stable and TMB was low at 4 mut/Mb. Notably, AR amplification was identified as a potential mechanism of resistance to prior AR-pathway therapy.

Given the above somatic landscape — specifically the AR amplification — and patient preference to continue on an oral AR-pathway agent before escalating to chemotherapy, the decision was made approximately 6 months ago to transition him from abiraterone/prednisone to enzalutamide 160 mg daily (Xtandi), with LHRH agonist continued. At his 3-month assessment, he had achieved a meaningful PSA decline from 8.6 to 2.3 ng/mL (approximately 73% reduction), with improvement in back pain to 1/10. He was tolerating enzalutamide well with only grade 1 fatigue and grade 1 hot flashes.

He now returns 6 months into enzalutamide therapy. Unfortunately, the PSA response has been lost. His most recent PSA today is 4.1 ng/mL, up from a nadir of approximately 1.9 ng/mL. His PSA doubling time over the past several measurements calculates to approximately 2.8 months, which is a concerning rate of rise. In addition, he now reports worsening low back pain, which he rates as 4/10 today (compared to 1/10 at his last visit), and new right hip pain that began approximately 3 to 4 weeks ago. He describes the hip pain as a deep, aching discomfort, rated 3/10 at rest but up to 5/10 with ambulation. He is not taking any opioids but has been using naproxen with only partial relief.

He denies any new neurologic symptoms — no leg weakness, no bowel or bladder dysfunction, no saddle anesthesia. He denies fever, chills. He reports mild fatigue (grade 1-2) and reduced exercise tolerance compared to 3 months ago. No new urinary symptoms. He does note a slight decrease in appetite but no significant weight loss since last visit. He denies hematuria, hemoptysis, or abdominal pain.

The pattern of rising PSA with a brisk doubling time, new bone pain involving the right hip, and pain in a site consistent with the right iliac crest/femoral region (a known area of prior metastatic disease) raises significant concern for biochemical and early clinical/radiographic progression on enzalutamide. Given his BRCA2 biallelic loss, the planned next step — if imaging confirms progression — is transition to olaparib (Lynparza), which carries FDA approval for HRR-deficient mCRPC based on the PROfound trial data showing PFS and OS benefit specifically in BRCA1/2-altered patients.

Importantly, while AR amplification identified on somatic NGS provides a potential mechanism for enzalutamide resistance, the BRCA2 biallelic loss remains the most actionable alteration at this juncture, making PARP inhibitor therapy the preferred next line.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1-2; decreased appetite; no significant unintentional weight loss reported at this visit; no fever or chills
- Musculoskeletal: Worsening low back pain 4/10 (lumbar), new right hip pain 3-5/10; no joint swelling
- Neurologic: Denies lower extremity weakness, numbness, bowel or bladder changes; no new headaches; no seizures (pertinent negative given prior enzalutamide use)
- GI: Mild anorexia; no nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation; no abdominal pain; no blood in stool
- GU: No hematuria; no dysuria; baseline obstructive urinary symptoms unchanged from prior (mild, pre-existing BPH-related)
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, lower extremity edema; no dyspnea at rest
- Respiratory: No cough, shortness of breath
- Skin: No rash; well-healed injection site from recent leuprolide depot
- Endocrine: Ongoing hot flashes (grade 1, pre-existing from ADT, not worsening)
- Psychiatric: Mildly anxious about disease status today; denies depression per PHQ-2 screen

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 134/82 mmHg
- Heart rate: 78 bpm, regular
- Temperature: 36.8°C
- Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
- O2 saturation: 98% on room air
- Weight: 72.1 kg (stable from last visit)
- Height: 175 cm
- BMI: 23.5
- ECOG Performance Status: 1-2 (ambulating without assistance but limited by pain; able to manage most activities of daily living independently)

General: Alert and oriented x3. Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Appears mildly uncomfortable when repositioning on the exam table. Mood cooperative but understandably anxious.

Head/Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No lymphadenopathy in cervical or supraclavicular chains. No facial plethora or temporal wasting.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No dullness to percussion. No accessory muscle use.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly on palpation. No right upper quadrant tenderness. Bowel sounds present and normoactive.

Musculoskeletal/Spine: Midline lumbar tenderness to deep palpation at L3-L4 levels, reproducible. Paraspinal muscle tenderness bilateral. Right hip: tenderness over the greater trochanter region and proximal femur with deep palpation; pain with passive internal rotation of the right hip (limited range of motion compared with left). No erythema, warmth, or swelling over the right hip. Bilateral lower extremity strength 5/5 proximally and distally. Reflexes 2+ bilateral patella and Achilles. Sensation intact to light touch bilateral lower extremities. Gait slightly antalgic on the right.

Genitourinary: Deferred (prostate exam not indicated at this visit given known metastatic disease and ongoing ADT).

Skin: No suspicious new lesions. No jaundice. Mild skin changes consistent with chronic androgen deprivation (decreased body hair).

Lymph nodes: No palpable inguinal, axillary, or supraclavicular adenopathy.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY):

- PSA: 4.1 ng/mL (prior values: nadir 1.9, last visit 2.8, now 4.1 — clear rising trend; PSA doubling time ~2.8 months)
- Testosterone: <10 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)
- Hemoglobin: 11.6 g/dL (mild anemia; stable from last visit; consistent with ADT effect)
- WBC: 6.4 K/uL
- Platelets: 184 K/uL
- Creatinine: 1.3 mg/dL (eGFR approximately 52 mL/min, chronically reduced — important for future therapy selection)
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- ALT: 28 U/L, AST: 24 U/L, Total bilirubin: 0.8 mg/dL (LFTs within normal limits)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 148 U/L (mildly elevated; has been chronically elevated in setting of bone metastases — currently stable)
- LDH: 212 U/L (upper limit of normal; not significantly elevated)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

Most recent imaging: Tc-99m bone scintigraphy and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained 3 months ago (at 6-month enzalutamide mark) showed stable osseous disease at L3, L4, right iliac crest, and right sacral ala without new lesions; no visceral metastases; PSA at that time was 2.8 ng/mL with early upward trend from nadir.

Given today's presentation — rising PSA with a doubling time of ~2.8 months, worsening back pain, and new right hip pain — urgent repeat bone scintigraphy and cross-sectional imaging is now being ordered (see plan below).

---

ASSESSMENT:

Mr. Kowalski is a 70-year-old male with metastatic CRPC, Gleason 9 (Grade Group 5), germline and somatic BRCA2 biallelic loss, with AR amplification identified on somatic tumor profiling, now approximately 6 months into second-line therapy with enzalutamide. He has had an initial partial PSA response followed by loss of response, with PSA now rising with a doubling time of approximately 2.8 months. He also has new right hip pain that is anatomically suspicious for a new or worsening bone metastasis in the right femoral neck/intertrochanteric region, a pattern consistent with disease progression beyond the known right iliac crest lesion.

1. Likely PSA progression on enzalutamide: PSA has risen from a nadir of 1.9 ng/mL to 4.1 ng/mL over approximately 2-3 months, with a PSA doubling time of ~2.8 months. While this alone meets biochemical progression criteria per PCWG3, we are awaiting radiographic confirmation before formalizing the transition of therapy. The AR amplification identified on somatic profiling at the time of CRPC conversion is a biologically plausible mechanism of acquired enzalutamide resistance, as AR amplification can drive continued AR signaling even in the presence of second-generation AR antagonists.

2. New right hip pain, clinically suspicious for new osseous metastasis: The right hip pain is deep, aching, worse with ambulation, and anatomically overlapping with the right iliac crest region (a known site of disease). New involvement of the right femoral neck or intertrochanteric region would represent radiographic disease progression. This is urgent to characterize both for treatment planning and for fracture risk assessment — femoral neck lesions with cortical involvement carry a risk of pathologic fracture that may warrant orthopedic surgery or radiation oncology consultation.

3. Management plan — awaiting imaging confirmation before formal transition:
   - Urgent bone scintigraphy (Tc-99m whole body bone scan) ordered today. If a new right hip lesion is identified with concern for cortical involvement, orthopedic oncology referral will be placed.
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis also ordered to evaluate for visceral metastases (liver, lung), given that visceral spread would substantially change the urgency and risk/benefit discussion around next-line therapy.
   - If imaging confirms radiographic progression (new bone lesions or any visceral disease), we will formally discontinue enzalutamide and proceed to olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg orally twice daily, which is FDA-approved for HRR-deficient mCRPC. Mr. Kowalski's BRCA2 biallelic somatic loss (with germline predisposition) is the strongest predictive biomarker of response to PARP inhibitor therapy, as demonstrated in the PROfound trial where BRCA1/2-altered patients derived the most robust benefit in both PFS and OS compared to the non-BRCA HRR subgroup.
   - Note: Bone-targeted therapy (denosumab or zoledronic acid) should be formally initiated at the time of next-line therapy given the progressive bone involvement. Renal function (current eGFR ~52 mL/min) will need to be factored in — zoledronic acid requires dose adjustment below GFR 60; denosumab has no renal dose adjustment requirement and may be preferred.

4. Pain management: Today's prescription for naproxen has been insufficient. Given the worsening pain with ambulation and the risk of a bony lesion in the weight-bearing right hip, I am concerned about continued NSAID monotherapy. Patient counseled to minimize weight-bearing on the right hip until imaging is available. We are prescribing low-dose oxycodone 5 mg every 6 hours as needed for breakthrough bone pain, with a plan to reassess after imaging. He was counseled on bowel regimen (senna/docusate) and safe opioid use. Radiation oncology palliative consultation will be considered if a localized painful bone lesion is confirmed on imaging.

5. ECOG PS: Now 1-2, representing a slight decline from prior visits. This is being noted carefully, as ECOG PS is a key factor in future therapy eligibility (particularly for PARP inhibitors, which are generally well-tolerated in PS 0-2 patients) and in the event further dose-intensive therapy is considered.

6. Ongoing ADT: Leuprolide acetate depot continued — next injection due in approximately 6 weeks. Testosterone confirmed castrate today.

7. Genetic counseling: Patient was previously informed of germline BRCA2 implications. His adult children have been advised to pursue cascade testing. No new action required at this visit.

8. Goals of care: Briefly reviewed today. Mr. Kowalski remains in good spirits overall and is engaged in his care. He understands the nature of his disease and the expected trajectory. He is motivated to pursue active treatment. We will continue to incorporate his preferences, quality-of-life priorities, and family support into decision-making as we move forward. Palliative care co-management will be considered if the disease trajectory continues to worsen or if pain becomes more difficult to manage.

---

PLAN SUMMARY:

1. Urgent Tc-99m whole body bone scan — ordered today; results expected within 48-72 hours.
2. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast — ordered today (concurrent with bone scan if scheduling permits).
3. If imaging confirms radiographic progression: discontinue enzalutamide and initiate olaparib 300 mg PO BID (Lynparza) for BRCA2-deficient mCRPC. Add denosumab 120 mg SQ q4 weeks (preferred over zoledronic acid given renal function) with calcium and vitamin D supplementation.
4. Oxycodone 5 mg q6h PRN for bone pain — new prescription provided; patient educated on use.
5. Orthopedic oncology referral to be placed if right hip femoral neck lesion confirmed on bone scan — assess for pathologic fracture risk.
6. Radiation oncology palliative consultation to be placed if painful localized lesion confirmed.
7. Continue leuprolide acetate — next injection at scheduled interval.
8. Repeat labs at next visit: PSA, testosterone, CBC, CMP.
9. Follow-up scheduled: Patient will return in approximately 2 weeks for imaging review and next-step treatment planning. If there is urgent concern from imaging (e.g., cortical destruction at femoral neck), patient instructed to call immediately.

Time spent in face-to-face counseling and coordination: approximately 45 minutes.

— Dictated and signed by Attending Oncologist, Genitourinary Medical Oncology
